## **ENGINEERING MODEL FOR DESIGN OF EXPLOSIVE FRAGMENTATION MUNITIONS**

- **Affiliation:** U.S. Army ARDEC, Armaments Engineering & Technology Center (AETC), Picatinny Arsenal, NJ

- **Distribution Statement:** Approved for public release; distribution is unlimited.

## **Page 2: Disclaimer and Destruction Notice**

The views, opinions, and/or findings contained in this report are those of the author(s) and should not be construed as an official Department of the Army position, policy, or decision, unless so designated by other documentation.

The citation in this report of the names of commercial firms or commercially available products or services does not constitute official endorsement by or approval of the U.S. Government.

**Destruction Notice:** Destroy this report when no longer needed by any method that will prevent disclosure of its contents or reconstruction of the document. Do not return to the originator.

## **Page 3: Standard Form 298 (Report Documentation)**

- **Report Date:** February 2007
- **Report Type:** Technical Report
- **Title:** Engineering Model for Design of Explosive Fragmentation Munitions
- **Author:** Dr. Vladimir M. Gold
- **Performing Organization:** U.S. Army ARDEC, AETC, Energetics and Warheads (AMSRD-AAR-AEE), Picatinny Arsenal, NJ 07806-5000
- **Sponsoring Agency:** U.S. Army ARDEC, EM, Technical Research Center (AMSRD-AAR-EMK), Picatinny Arsenal, NJ 07806-5000
- **Abstract:** An engineering model for design of explosive fragmentation munitions presented in this work is based on integrating three-dimensional axisymmetric hydrocode analyses with analyses from a newly developed fragmentation computer code MOTT. The validation of the MOTT code fragmentation model was accomplished using the existing munition arena test data. After having established the crucial parameters of the model, a new explosive fragmentation munitions was designed and optimized. Upon fabrication of the developed munition, the performance of the new charge was tested in a series of small-scale experiments including the flash radiography, the high-speed photography, and the sawdust fragment recovery. Considering relative simplicity of the model, the accuracy of the MOTT code predictions is rather remarkable.
- **Subject Terms:** Fragmentation modeling, Natural fragmentation, ALACV, Fragmentation warhead, Airburst warhead M789, MOTT theory.
- **Number of Pages:** 23

## **Page 4: Acknowledgments**

The germinal concept of integrating the CALE hydrocode analyses and analytical fragmentation modeling was due to Dr. E. L. Baker of the U.S. Army Armament Research, Development and Engineering Center (ARDEC), Picatinny Arsenal, New Jersey.

K. W. Ng of ARDEC is acknowledged for his contributions in preparing the CALE model employed in these analyses.

W. J. Poulos and Dr. B. E. Fuchs both of ARDEC are acknowledged for obtaining the experimental data presented in this work.

The work was conducted under the auspices of the ALACV Warhead Program. Dr. E. L. Baker and J. M. Hirlinger of ARDEC are acknowledged for making this work possible.

- **Figure 3:** Fragment velocity distribution versus spray angle charge A. (Page 6)
- **Figure 4:** Cumulative number of fragments in the fragment spray versus the fragment size ![][image1]. (Page 7)
- **Figure 5:** Cumulative number of fragments versus ![][image1] for varying ![][image2] CALE-MOTT analyses equations 14 and 17 - charge A. (Page 8)
- **Figure 6:** Number of fragments in the fragment spray, varying the shell fragmentation time and the ![][image2] - charge A. (Page 8)
- **Figure 7:** CALE code modeling and experimentation - charge B. (Page 9)
- **Figure 8:** Development of surface fractures in the expanding shell - charge B. (Page 10)
- **Figure 9:** Fragment velocity distribution versus spray angle charge B. (Page 11)
- **Figure 10:** Cumulative number of fragments versus the fragment mass ![][image3] - charge B. (Page 12)

## **Page 6: Section 1 - Introduction**

A technique for predicting the performance of explosive fragmentation munitions presented in this work is based on integrating the CALE computer program (ref. 1) with a newly developed fragmentation computer code MOTT. CALE is a two-dimensional and three-dimensional axial symmetric high rate finite difference computer program based on the Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian formulation of the governing equations. The mathematical description of the MOTT code is given in the section that follows.

The geometries of two example problems are shown in **Figure 1**. Upon initiation of the high explosive charges, rapid expansion of high-pressure high-velocity detonation products results in high-strain high-strain-rate dilation of the hardened steel shells, which eventually rupture generating a "spray" of high-velocity steel fragments.

- **Charge A:** The dilation of the steel shell is accompanied by the implosion of the copper shaped charge liner that produces a high-speed metal jet moving along the charge's axis of symmetry ![][image4].
- **Charge B:** In addition to the explosive charge, the hardened steel shell also encapsulates a tracer and a fuze that occupy a significant volume of the munition payload. A threaded connection between the fuze and the fragmenting portion of the hardened steel case was also included in the CALE model. In order to allow slippage, the threaded joint was modeled with a few rows of computation cells employing ideally-plastic-zero-yield-strength material with the same hydrodynamic response parameters as steel. Following the expansion of the detonation products, the fuze section of the warhead is projected in the negative direction of the ![][image4]-axis, without contributing to the lethality of the fragment spray.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 1:**

- **Left Column (Charge A):** Shows the initial configuration at ![][image5] displaying a hardened steel shell, an internal explosive fill, and a copper shaped charge liner pointing inward. At ![][image6], expansion starts. At ![][image7], the steel fragments are thrown out at trajectory velocity vector ![][image8] and spray angle ![][image9] relative to the ![][image4]-axis, while a prominent copper jet propagates straight forward along the axis.
- **Right Column (Charge B):** Shows the initial state at ![][image5] highlighting a tracer cavity and fuze payload separated by a threaded joint from the main explosive chamber. At ![][image10], expansion progresses. By ![][image11], the steel fragments expand outward at spray angle ![][image9] while the fuze and tracer components project rearward along the negative ![][image4]-axis.

## **Page 7: Materials Modeling & Section 2 - Mott Code Fragmentation Model**

In addition to the specification of the problem geometry and initial and boundary conditions, equations of states and constitutive equations for all materials have to be specified before the solution procedure can be initiated.

- **Explosive Modeling:** Modeled using the Jones-Wilkins-Lee-Baker equation of state employing a set of parameters resulting from thermo-chemical equilibrium analyses of detonation products with the JAGUAR code and calibrated with copper cylinder test expansion data.
- **Metal Response:** The hydrodynamic responses of the steel shell and the copper liner were modeled using a standard linear polynomial approximation usually employed for metals. The constitutive behavior of these metals was modeled using the Steinberg-Guinan yield-strength model and the von Mises yielding criterion. A standard set of parameters available from Tipton (ref. 2) was employed in the analyses.

Since the extent of dilation of the rapidly expanding steel shell is limited by its strength, at some point the shell ruptures generating a spray of steel fragments moving with trajectories at angles with the ![][image4]-axis. Accordingly, the principal topic of this work is a numerical model for the analytical description of parameters of the resulting fragment spray as functions of the "spray" angle ![][image9].

In typical large-scale explosive fragmentation tests (arena tests), the tested munitions are positioned at the origin of the reference polar coordinate system and surrounded with a series of velocity-measuring screens and fragment-catching witness panels, all at significant distances from the warhead. Accordingly, the fragmentation characteristics are assessed as functions of polar angles ![][image12] identifying angular positions of these measuring devices. Assuming that the fragment trajectory angles do not change with time (that is, the lateral drift of fragments due to air resistance is small) and that definitions of angles ![][image9] and ![][image12] are approximately identical, the developed model enables prediction of crucial characteristics of explosive fragmenting munitions including the number of fragments, the fragment size distribution, and the average fragment velocities.

### **Mott Code Fragmentation Model**

For a large part, the MOTT code fragmentation model is based on the Mott's theory of break-up of cylindrical "ring-bombs" (ref. 3), in which the average length of the resulting circumferential fragments is a function of the radius and velocity of the ring at the moment of break-up, and the mechanical properties of the metal.

Following Mott and Linfoot (ref. 4), the "random variations" in fragment sizes are accounted for through the following fragment distribution relationship:

![][image13]*(Equation 1)*

Where:

- ![][image14] represents the total number of fragments of mass greater than ![][image3].
- ![][image15] is defined as one half of the average fragment mass.
- ![][image16] where ![][image17] is the total mass of the fragments.

In attempting to evaluate the distribution of fragment sizes occurring in the dynamic fragmentation of expanding metal rings, Mott (ref. 3) introduced an idealized model in which the average circumferential fragment lengths are not random, but determined by the interaction of stress release waves originating from instantaneous fractures in the body. A schematic of Mott's model is shown in **Figure 2(a)**.

## **Page 8: Figure 2 and Equation Explanations**

Assuming that a fracture in the ring is supposed to have occurred first at ![][image18] and that stress release waves have traveled to points ![][image19] and ![][image20], further fractures can no longer take place in regions ![][image21] and ![][image22]. On the other hand, in the regions ![][image23] and ![][image24] the plastic strain is increasing, which increases the probability of fractures at any point in these regions, especially at points ![][image19], ![][image25], ![][image20], and ![][image26]. Thus, according to Mott's theory, the average size of fragments is determined by the rate at which stress relieved regions ![][image21] and ![][image22] spread through the plastically expanding ring.

At the moment of fracture, let ![][image27] be the radius of the ring and ![][image28] be the velocity with which the shell is moving outwards. Then, according to Mott (ref. 3), the average circumferential length of the resulting fragments is:

![][image29]*(Equation 2)*

In **Equation 2**:

- ![][image30] and ![][image31] denote the density and the strength of the metal, respectively.
- ![][image2] is a semi-empirical statistical constant determining the dynamic fracture properties of the material.

Given that the shape and the average fragment lengths are known, the idealized average fragment mass can be calculated. For example, assuming approximate cubic-shaped fragments, ![][image15] takes the following form:

![][image32]*(Equation 3)*

A schematic for the newly developed technique implemented in the MOTT code is shown in **Figure 2(b)**. For computational purposes, the shell is discretized into a finite number of short "ring" segments, ![][image33]. For each discrete ring element, ![][image34] uniform field variables are assumed. Accordingly, the masses, the velocities, and radii of ring segments ![][image34] are defined by the mass averages of the respective parameters:

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 2:**

- **Diagram (a):** Explains the stress release model on a ring cross-section. Primary fractures occur at ![][image35]. Stress release waves travel symmetrically outward to ![][image36]. The shaded regions under ![][image21] represent stress-relieved areas where no further cracks can form, while plastic expansion continues on the rest of the circle, driving subsequent cracking.
- **Diagram (b):** Visualizes the coordinate discretization of the shell in polar space ![][image37], tracking a cell element at coordinate ![][image38], with slice angle ![][image39], cell radius ![][image40], and outward velocity vector ![][image41].

## **Page 9: Section 2 (Continued) - Discretization and Segment Equations**

The system of equations mapping the CALE cell parameters to the discrete ring elements is given as:

![][image42]*(Equation 4)*

*![][image43](Equation 5)*

*![][image44](Equation 6)*

Over the segment boundary condition:

![][image45]*(Equation 7)*

In **Equations 4 through 6**:

- ![][image46], ![][image47], and ![][image48] denote the mass, the velocity, and the radial coordinate of the ![][image49]-th computational cell from the CALE generated data.
- ![][image50] denotes the set of computational cells contained in the ![][image34]-th ring segment.
- ![][image51] denotes the polar angle that corresponds to the ![][image34]-th ring segment given by:

![][image52]*(Equation 8)*

For each computational cell ![][image49], the velocity ![][image47] and the angle ![][image53] are calculated, respectively, by:

![][image54]*(Equation 9)*

*![][image55](Equation 10)*

In **Equations 9 and 10**, ![][image56] and ![][image57] denote the axial and the radial velocity components from the CALE-code generated data.

Given that the velocities and the radii of ring segments ![][image34] are determined through equations 5 and 6, the resulting fragment size distributions in each segment ![][image34] can be calculated through the following relationships:

![][image58]*(Equation 11)*

*![][image59](Equation 12)*

## **Page 10: Section 2 (Continued) - Statistical Integration Models**

![][image60]*(Equation 13)*

As the detonation wave travels along the shell length and the expanding detonation products rupture the shell, in the case of the idealized "long-pipe-bombs," the break-up radii ![][image40] and the break-up velocities ![][image61] of the individual segments ![][image34] are approximately the same, regardless of the axial positions of the segments. Accordingly, taking ![][image62] the number of fragment distribution relationship is given by the original Mott's equation 1.

However, in the case of conventional explosive fragmentation munitions with shell geometries far from that of the idealized "long-pipe-bombs," the break-up radii ![][image40] and the break-up velocities ![][image61] vary along the shell length, so that the resulting variance in the average fragment half-weights ![][image63] of the individual segments ![][image34] may be rather significant.

The existence of significant differences in the average fragment sizes between the cylindrical and the curved portions of the shell has been experimentally confirmed in this work through flash radiography and high-speed photography. Accordingly, the following two fragment distribution relationships are introduced herein.

The **"shell-averaged"** fragment distribution is defined as:

![][image64]*(Equation 14)*

where ![][image65] and ![][image66] are defined as:

![][image67]*(Equation 15)*

*![][image68](Equation 16)*

The **"ring-segment-averaged"** fragment distribution is defined as:

![][image69]*(Equation 17)*

## **Page 11: Section 3 - Mott Code Validation: Charge A Analyses**

The validation of the MOTT code fragmentation model was accomplished using the existing Charge A arena test data. The fundamental assumption of all fragmentation analyses presented in this work was that the fragmentation occurs instantly throughout the entire body of the shell. Following Mott's critical fracture strain concept (ref. 3) and assuming that for a given shell geometry and materials, the shell fragmentation time is a function of the cumulative dilatational plastic strain in the shell, the shell fragmentation time can be conveniently expressed in terms of the global shell dilatational properties.

Given that in a typical fragmentation munition device the explosive is tightly confined inside the shell, the cumulative strains of the expanding explosive and the surrounding shell are nearly proportional. Accordingly, the critical fracture strain at the moment of the shell break-up may be conveniently measured in terms of the high explosive detonation products volume expansions, ![][image70].

**Figure 3** shows the effect of the shell fragmentation time on the fragment spray velocity distribution function. The seemingly significant disagreement between the experimental velocities and the analyses for ![][image71] is due to deliberate omission of the shaped charge jet data from the MOTT fragmentation analyses, mainly because of the minimal contribution to the overall fragment-spray lethality. Accordingly, the copper shaped charge jet was neglected in all fragmentation analyses, although included in the CALE model in order to maintain proper explosive confinement parameters.

As shown in **Figure 3**, varying the shell fragmentation time from approximately ![][image72] (at which the detonation products had expanded approximately three times its original volume, ![][image73]) to approximately ![][image74] (![][image75]), the resulting changes in the fragment spray angles ![][image9] were rather small, while the fragment spray velocities were affected rather significantly. As shown in Figure 3, delaying the moment of the shell break-up predicts considerable increases in the fragment spray velocities, apparently due to the prolonged "pressurized" interaction with expanding detonation products that increased the total momentum transferred to the shell.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 3:**

- A plot of velocity (cm/![][image15]s) on the y-axis against ![][image76] (degrees) on the x-axis.
- Shows three curves: CALE-MOTT at ![][image74] (![][image77]), CALE-MOTT at ![][image72] (![][image78]), and experimental data points.
- The experimental data has a very close fit with the ![][image72] curves across the ![][image79] to ![][image80] range, peaking around ![][image81] (at ![][image82]) and ![][image83] (at ![][image84]).

## **Page 12: Section 3 (Continued) - Cumulative Mass Fits**

**Figures 4 through 6** show plots of the number of fragments in the fragment spray as functions of the fragment size ![][image1], the choice of the number of fragment distribution function model (Eqs. 14 and 17), the spray angle ![][image9], the shell fragmentation time, and the dynamic fracture parameter ![][image2]. As shown in the figures, increases in the parameter ![][image2] resulted in increases of the number of fragments ![][image33], both for the ![][image85] and the ![][image86] relationships. These results are in agreement with Mott's theory (ref. 3), according to which the parameter ![][image2] defines the probability of fracture in the plastically expanding shell determining the number of breaks in the circumferential direction.

**Figure 4** shows a plot of a series of curves given by Equation 14, ![][image87], with all analyses repeated for two parameters considered: the shell fragmentation time assumed and the dynamic fracture constant, ![][image2]. For example, taking the ![][image72] (![][image88]) fragmentation time with ![][image89] and the ![][image74] (![][image75]) fragmentation time with ![][image90] resulted in nearly identical fragment distribution curves, both in good agreement with the data.

The accepted shell fragmentation time was determined from the high-speed photographic data of Pearson (ref. 5). Following Pearson, the fragmentation of shells with idealized cylindrical geometries occurs approximately at 3 volume expansions, the instant of fragmentation defined as the time at which the detonation products first appear emanating from the fractures in the shell. Accordingly, the accepted shell fragmentation time was approximately ![][image72] (![][image88]).

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 4:**

- The y-axis shows normalized fragments (![][image91]) ranging from 0.0 to 1.5. The x-axis shows normalized mass (![][image1]) from 0 to 25.
- The curve with ![][image89] at ![][image72] traces a sharp decay curve that aligns exceptionally well with the triangular experimental data points.

## **Page 13: Section 3 (Continued) - Equation Comparisons**

**Figure 5** shows a plot of the cumulative number of fragments from the arena test data and the CALE-MOTT analytic predictions employing ![][image87] and ![][image92] relationships; i.e., the overall "shell-averaged" fragment size distribution (Eq. 14) and the "ring-segment-averaged" fragment size distribution (Eq. 17), respectively.

As shown in the plot, employing Equation 14 resulted in consistently higher values of the cumulative numbers of fragments than that of Equation 17, apparently because of the nature of the definition of the overall "shell-average" fragment mass ![][image66] (Eq. 16). Two equally reasonable fits were obtained for both relationships, resulting in ![][image89] for Equation 14 and in ![][image93] for Equation 17 with standard deviations of approximately ![][image94] and ![][image95], respectively.

Both curves were fitted at a single point ![][image96], which corresponds to the total number of fragments with mass greater than 3 grains; the aim was to replicate the overall lethality of the fragmenting spray, rather than focusing on the entire range of the ![][image1] values considered. Accordingly, the accepted shell fragmentation time of approximately ![][image72] (![][image88]) and two values ![][image89] and ![][image93] were selected for all further analyses.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 5:**

- Compares Eq 14 and Eq 17 curves for ![][image89] and ![][image93].
- Explains the single point matching constraint at ![][image97] (![][image96]) where the curves cross.
- Clearly demonstrates that the ring-segment-averaged curve (Eq 17) fits lower than the shell-averaged curve (Eq 14) at higher mass values.

**Figure 6** shows a plot of the number of fragments with mass greater than 3 grains versus the spray angle ![][image9], which is the principal lethality parameter of the fragment spray of the munition.

- **Discrepancy at ![][image98]:** Due to fragments from the shaped charge liner-retaining ring, which was not included in the CALE model.
- **Discrepancy at ![][image99]:** Due to fragments from a rotating band that had not been included in the CALE model.

Despite these exclusions, the overall agreement is excellent.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 6:**

- Plots number of fragments (![][image91]) against ![][image9] (degrees) from ![][image100] to ![][image80].
- Displays a prominent spike at ![][image81] representing the primary radial spray direction, which matches the experimental data peak closely.

## **Page 14: Section 4 - Charge B Modeling and Experimentation**

After having established the crucial parameters of the model, Charge B (shown in Figure 1) was designed and optimized. Upon fabrication, the performance of the new charge was tested in a series of experiments including flash radiography, high-speed photography, and sawdust fragment recovery.

- **Flash Radiography Tests:** Performed using two 150 kV x-ray heads located approximately 74 in. in front of the round. Shortly after initiating the round, each of the two x-ray heads was flashed at separate prescribed times and intervals several microseconds apart. Two flash radiography tests were conducted, each resulting in two dynamic images of the expanding fragmented steel shell, both images superimposed on the film.
- **High-Speed Photography Tests:** Performed employing a Cordin Framing Camera, Model No. 121, capable of recording up to 26 high-speed exposure frames with time intervals between individual frames of less than ![][image101] apart. The round was placed on a test stand in front of a fiducial grid, surrounded with four Argon gas light bombs, all enclosed in a white paper tent. A total of two high-speed photography tests were conducted, each resulting in over 20 dynamic images.

**Figure 7** shows a comparison between the CALE code predictions and the images of the expanding and partially fractured shell. The model resulted in an accurate prediction of the shape of the expanding hardened steel shell, including the early break-out of the detonation products through the joint between the fuze and the main charge.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 7:**

- Three-panel graphic:
  1. CALE analysis showing computer-simulated expansion profiles.
  1. Flash radiograph at ![][image102] and ![][image103] showing crack patterns and escaping gases.
  1. High-speed framing image at ![][image102] capturing the gas plumes.
- Notes highlight the "Early break-up" at the threaded base joint, and "Detonation products breakthrough through cracks".

## **Page 15: Section 4 (Continued) - Fracture Evolution Details**

Referring to the flash radiographic image of the partially fragmented shell given in **Figure 7**, it is important to note a remarkable difference between shapes and sizes of fragments ejected from the cylindrical and the curved portions of the charge. As seen from both the ![][image102] and from the ![][image103] radiographic images, the majority of cracks in the cylindrical portion of the charge are in the axial direction, resulting in a fragment spray with relatively large axially oriented splinter-like fragments. On the contrary, in the curved nose portion of the shell, the orientation of cracks is random and the distances between fissures are shorter, resulting in a fragment spray of predominantly small compactly shaped fragments.

**Figure 8** shows a series of high-speed photographic images of the condition of the surface as the hardened steel shell expands and the detonation products emerge through the cracks. As shown in the figure, visible fractures start to appear on the surface of the shell at approximately ![][image104] after the detonation, which according to the CALE code analyses corresponds to approximately ![][image105].

Examination of the entire series of images taken approximately ![][image101] apart shows that as the shell expands, fractures develop first in the cylindrical portion of the shell. As the shell continues to expand, the developed fractures propagate mostly in the axial direction, occasionally linking up by new cross-cracks in the circumferential direction, ultimately resulting in the formation of large splinter-like fragments shown in the radiographic images of Figure 7.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 8:**

- A plot of ![][image70] expansion on the y-axis against Time (![][image15]s) on the x-axis.
- Inset photographs of the warhead are mapped to key milestone points on the curve:
  - ![][image106] (![][image107]): Axial cracking starts.
  - ![][image108] (![][image109]): Gases visibly break through and appear on the surface.
  - A threshold line is marked at ![][image110], representing the average analytical fragmentation event.

## **Page 16: Section 4 (Continued) - Expansion and Velocity Analyses**

As the shell continues to expand, fractures gradually advance towards the curved nosed portion of the shell, and at approximately ![][image111], or at approximately ![][image112] volume expansions, the entire shell is fully fragmented, the fragmentation being defined as the instant at which the detonation products first appear as they emanate from the fractures in the shell.

Following the CALE-MOTT model assumption that the critical fracture strain at the moment of the shell break-up is expressed in terms of the high explosive detonation products volume expansions, the "average" volume expansion at the time of the shell break-up is then approximately one-half of the value of volume expansions of the fully fragmented shell, hence ![][image113]. It is interesting to note that the value of ![][image113] is in excellent agreement with that assumed initially based on the high-speed photographic data of Pearson (ref. 5).

**Figure 9** shows plots of CALE-MOTT analytic predictions of the fragment velocity distribution function and the experimental data. The experimental data considered here was reduced from the radiographic images of the expanding and partially fractured shell. As shown in the figure, two analytical fragment velocity distribution functions were considered:

1. Assuming that the entire shell fragments instantly at approximately at ![][image114] (![][image73]).
1. Assuming that the entire shell fragments instantly at approximately at ![][image115] (![][image116]).

Given that the explosive was modeled using a semi-empirical set of parameters calibrated with the experimental copper cylinder expansion data, the CALE predictions of the expanding (but not fractured) shell velocities should be reasonably accurate. As shown in the figure, the agreement between the data and the ![][image78] curve is significantly better than with the ![][image117] curve, suggesting that the shell fractured at approximately 3 volume expansions. In addition, given that (according to the high-speed photography) the entire shell had fully fractured at approximately 6.2 volume expansions, the assumption of the ![][image73] instantaneous fracture event is quite reasonable: once the detonation products start to escape through the cracks, any further gains in the velocities are relatively small and may be neglected.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 9:**

- Plots velocity (cm/![][image15]s) against spray angle ![][image9] (![][image100] to ![][image80]).
- Compares experimental data sets from Test 4-812 against analytical curves for ![][image114] and ![][image115].
- The ![][image114] (![][image78]) curve perfectly matches the experimental plateau at ![][image118] across the ![][image119] sector before dipping to ![][image120] near ![][image121].

## **Page 17: Section 4 (Continued) - Sawdust Recovery and Mass Curve**

The fragment recovery tests considered in this work were performed employing disposable plastic containers measuring approximately 52 in. in diameter and 59 in. in height, filled with approximately 1000 lbs of sawdust. After inserting the tested round in an inflatable rubber balloon and filling the balloon with air, the balloon was positioned in the sawdust in the middle of the plastic container. After detonating the round, the resulting fragments were recovered employing a combination of the magnetic (for separating fragments from the sawdust) and the vacuum (for separating sawdust from the fragments) recovery techniques.

The mass distribution of the collected fragments was analyzed employing an electronic high precision balance gauge interfaced with a computer system capable of automatic counting of fragments as they were placed on the scale and weighed. Weighing of the fragments was performed employing the Ohaus Voyager Balance model V14130 gauge with a maximum capacity of 410 g and precision of 0.001 g. A total of two sawdust recovery tests were performed, each resulting in successful recovery of approximately over 99.8% of the mass of the steel shell.

**Figure 10** shows plots of CALE-MOTT analytic predictions of the cumulative number of fragments compared with the data from the fragment recovery tests. Two analytic relationships had been considered:

1. The **"shell-averaged"** fragment size distribution (Eq. 14).
1. The **"ring-segment-averaged"** fragment size distribution (Eq. 17).

As shown in the figure, the analytic prediction of Equation 14 significantly disagrees with the experimental data, regardless of the value ![][image2] considered. The disagreement between the Equation 14 predictions and the data is mostly because of the significant variance in fragment weights ![][image63] along the shell, ultimately resulting in over-predicting the "shell-averaged" fragment weight ![][image66] (Eq. 16).

On the contrary, the agreement between the data and the ![][image93] "ring-segment-averaged" fragment size distribution given by Equation 17 is quite good: ![][image122]. Given the relative simplicity of the model, the overall agreement between the analyses and the data is excellent.

#### **Visual Annotation for Figure 10:**

- Plots cumulative normalized fragments on the y-axis against fragment mass in grams on the x-axis (![][image123] to ![][image124] grams).
- Compares experimental curves from Test 4-823 and 4-810.
- Equation 14 (shell-averaged) severely over-predicts fragment mass, shifting the decay curve far to the right.
- Equation 17 (ring-segment-averaged) with ![][image93] tracks the experimental decay perfectly.

## **Page 18: Section 5 - Conclusions**

A technique for predicting the performance of explosive fragmentation munitions presented in this work is based on integrating three-dimensional axisymmetric hydrocode analyses with analyses from a newly developed fragmentation computer code MOTT. The validation of the MOTT code fragmentation model was accomplished using the existing munition arena test data.

After having established the crucial parameters of the model, a new explosive fragmentation munition was designed and optimized. Upon fabrication of the developed munition, the performance of the new charge was tested in a series of small-scale experiments including flash radiography, high-speed photography, and sawdust fragment recovery.

Considering the relative simplicity of the model, the accuracy of the MOTT code predictions is rather remarkable.

## **Page 19: Section 6 - References**

1. Tipton, R. E., "CALE Users Manual", Version 910201, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, 1991.
1. Tipton, R. E., "EOS Coefficients for the CALE Code for Some Materials", Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, 1991.
1. Mott, N. F., F.R.S., "Fragmentation of Steel Cases", *Proc. Roy. Soc.*, 189, pp. 300-308, 1947.
1. Mott, N. F. and Linfoot, E. H., "A theory of fragmentation", Ministry of Supply, A.C. 3348, January 1943.
1. Pearson, J., "A Fragmentation Model for Cylindrical Warheads", Technical Report NWC TP 7124, Naval Weapons Center, China Lake, California, December 1990.

## **Page 20: Section 7 - Distribution List**

- **U.S. Army ARDEC**
  - ATTN: AMSRD-AAR-EMK
  - AMSRD-AAR-GC
  - AMARD-AAR-AEE-W, E. Baker
  - V. Gold (5 copies)
  - AMSRD-AAR-AEM-J, J. Hirlinger
  - G. Flemming
  - AMSRD-AAR-AEW-M(D), S. Liss
  - G. Moshier
  - AMSRD-AAR-AEM-D, G. Voorhis
  - *Location:* Picatinny Arsenal, NJ 07806-5000
- **Defense Technical Information Center (DTIC)**
  - ATTN: Accessions Division
  - 8725 John J. Kingman Road, Ste 0944
  - Fort Belvoir, VA 22060-6218
- **Commander, Soldier and Biological/Chemical Command**
  - ATTN: AMSSB-CII, Library
  - Aberdeen Proving Ground, MD 21010-5423

  - ATTN: AMSRL-CI-LP, Technical Library, Bldg. 4600
  - Aberdeen Proving Ground, MD 21005-5066
- **Chief, Benet Weapons Laboratory, AETC**
  - U.S. Army Research, Development and Engineering Command
  - Armament Research, Development and Engineering Center
  - ATTN: AMSRD-AAR-AEW
  - Watervliet, NY 12189-5000

  - ATTN: ATRC-WSS-R
  - White Sands Missile Range, NM 88002
- **Chemical Propulsion Information Agency**
  - ATTN: Accessions
  - 10630 Little Patuxent Parkway, Suite 202
  - Columbia, MD 21044-3204

  - 600 Second Street, NE
